page_content:
# Passage:
The natural development and repair of bone tissue provides a good example for the design of mineralized tissue fabrication schemes. Besides cells, bone tissue consists of both inorganic and organic components. The inorganic component is a carbonated, calcium-deficient, nano-crystalline hydroxyapatite[HA = Ca5(PO4)3(OH)]. The organic component is a matrix composed of type I collagen (95%) and non-collageneous proteins.  
Researchers in tissue engineering have developed apeptide amphiphile(Compound**1**, Figure 1) that can be used to make a nanostructured fibrous scaffold(composite)similar to an extracellular matrix found in bone.  
![CP-9-1-202CP_76325-00P1.gif](https://s3.amazonaws.com/wmx-api-production/courses/1657/images/CP-9-1-202CP_76325-00P1.gif)  
Compound**1**  
|**Figure 1**|Structure of peptide amphiphile, Compound**1**, used to build scaffolds for synthetic bone formation|  
Compound**1**contained five regions: (1) ahydrophobic region, (2) residues that could becross-linkedfor additional structural integrity, (3)spacer residues,(4)a residue that was phosphorylatedfor future attachment of nanocrystals of HA, and (5) residues with affinity for cells.  
Molecules of Compound**1**spontaneously self-assemble intocylindrical micellesin water atpH 4but freelyredissolve at pH 8(Equation 1). Oxidation of self-assembled,micellular Compound**1**with iodineresulted in asolid material(Compound**2**) that wasstable to alkaline solutions(Equation 2). Reaction ofCompound**2**with dithiol(Compound**3**) resulted inreformation of Compound**1**(Equation 3).  
![CP-9-1-202CP_76325-00P2.gif](https://s3.amazonaws.com/wmx-api-production/courses/1657/images/CP-9-1-202CP_76325-00P2.gif)  
**Equation 1**  
![CP-9-1-202CP_76325-00P3.gif](https://s3.amazonaws.com/wmx-api-production/courses/1657/images/CP-9-1-202CP_76325-00P3.gif)  
**Equation 2**  
![CP-9-1-202CP_76325-00P4.gif](https://s3.amazonaws.com/wmx-api-production/courses/1657/images/CP-9-1-202CP_76325-00P4.gif)  
**Equation 3**  
The researchers wanted to mimic the alignment that occurs between crystals of HA and collagen fibrils in bone tissue. They created a peptide amphiphile that assembles into cylindrical micelles as shown in Figure 2. Subsequent capture of nanocrystals of HA followed by mineralization was used to form synthetic bone.  
![CP-9-1-202CP_76325-00P5.gif](https://s3.amazonaws.com/wmx-api-production/courses/1657/images/CP-9-1-202CP_76325-00P5.gif)  
Figure 2Space-filling molecular model illustrating cylindrical micelle formation by the peptide amphiphile, Compound 1
